title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
How to start Investment with your First Salary: Step-by-Step Guide
Why Start Investing from Your First Salary?
Wealth creation rests on the powerful principle of compounding. The sooner you begin investing, the longer your money and the returns it generates can compound. Over time, it’s not market timing but the passage of years that works in favour of the investor. When you invest your first salary, you can let your money start working for you early, giving you a significant advantage in building wealth. So, let’s know how to start investment with first salary.
Step-by-Step Guide: How to Start Investing with Your First Salary
If you are wondering how to start investing your first salary, here are some points that can help -
Step 1: Protect Yourself with Life and Health Insurance Life insurance may not feel urgent when you’re young, healthy, and single, but buying early offers significant long- term advantages. Premiums are much lower when you start young, and a term insurance plan can provide high coverage at an affordable cost. As you move into your 30s, you can reassess your needs and adjust your policy to protect a growing family or dependent parents. Life insurance acts as a vital safety net, ensuring your loved ones are financially secure if the unexpected happens.
A health insurance plan, on the other hand, can cover medical bills and give you financial relief.
By investing in life and health insurance early, you can safeguard your family’s future, giving them peace of mind and stability when they need it most.
Step 2: Build an Emergency Fund Before Investing An emergency fund acts as a buffer against unexpected events such as medical bills, sudden home repairs, or vehicle breakdowns, helping you avoid dipping into savings or taking on debt. This fund ensures you can cover costs without any additional financial strain. Experts suggest maintaining an emergency fund worth at least 10 times your monthly income (subject to individual preferences) to protect your financial stability and gain peace of mind during life’s uncertainties.
Step 3: Define Your Short-Term and Long-Term Financial Goals Investments can be largely divided into two categories based on the purpose behind them:
Short-term financial goals are those you aim to accomplish within one to three years. Common examples include setting aside money for a vacation, buying a new car, or creating an emergency fund. Since these goals require quick access to funds, they are best supported by low-risk investments that maintain liquidity.
Long-term financial goals extend beyond three years and often involve larger commitments such as saving for retirement, financing a child’s education, or purchasing a home. Because these goals allow more time to grow, they can benefit from a more aggressive investment strategy, giving you the ability to weather market ups and downs while building wealth.
Step 4: Understand Your Risk Appetite Understanding your personal risk appetite should be one of the first steps in your investment journey. Evaluating risk means taking into account key financial factors such as inflation, market volatility, interest rates, your investment time horizon, and how your assets are allocated. By identifying your own tolerance for risk, you’ll be better equipped to make informed decisions and choose investments that align with your financial goals and comfort level.
Step 5: Decide How Much You Can Invest Every Month Managing your earnings effectively begins with creating a budget, as it helps you track where your money is going each month. A simple and popular method for beginners is the 50-30-20 rule, which divides your income into three clear categories: 50% for essential needs such as rent, loan EMIs, groceries, and fuel; 30% for personal wants like entertainment, dining out, or leisure activities; and 20% for savings to secure your financial future. By following this approach, you can strike a healthy balance between meeting daily expenses, enjoying life, and building long-term financial security through different types of investment plans.
Step 6:  Start with Simple and Goal-Based Investment Options There are diverse range of investment avenues, each carrying its own balance of risk and potential reward. Gaining a clear understanding of these choices is essential for making smart, well-informed financial decisions.
Step 7: Invest Regularly and Stay Consistent Instead of trying to predict the perfect time to invest, a smarter approach is to keep adding money to your account on a regular basis, whether it’s with every paycheque, once a month, or any other routine that works for you. This steady habit keeps you invested through market ups and downs and takes away the stress of timing the market. Over time, those consistent contributions combine with the power of compounding, where your returns start earning returns of their own. The result is that even small, regular deposits can grow into a substantial amount, making a noticeable difference to your wealth in the long run.
Step 8:  Review and Increase Investments as your Investments Grow As your financial goals and priorities evolve, it’s important to keep your investments in sync. Make it a habit to review your portfolio at least once a year to confirm that it still reflects your objectives. If necessary, rebalance your holdings to maintain the asset allocation that best supports your long- term strategy and risk tolerance.
Common Mistakes to Avoid with Your First Investments
Steering clear of the following-mentioned frequent mistakes can significantly boost your long-term financial growth, even if your monthly income is very modest.
No Emergency Cushion:
Jumping into investments without first setting aside an emergency fund can backfire. In a crisis, you may be forced to withdraw investments, disrupting the compounding process.
Taking Pause During Market Falls:
Market downturns are actually the best time for investments like SIPs and Mutual funds, since you buy more units at lower prices. Halting contributions during dips means missing out on this advantage.
Following Past Performance Blindly:
Picking funds only because they did well previously is a risky move. Focus instead on whether the fund suits your financial goals and risk tolerance.
Not Scaling Up Investments:
Keeping the same investment amount for years limits growth. As your income increases, your investment contributions should rise too.
Owning Too Many Funds:
Spreading money across too many varied investments often leads to duplication rather than true diversification.
Waiting for the “Perfect” Entry Point:
Delaying investments in hopes of better timing usually results in lost opportunities. Consistency matters more than timing.
Key Takeaways
Begin early to harness the power of compounding because time, not timing, is the biggest wealth creator.
Secure life and health insurance early; it’s cheaper when you’re young and ensures your family’s financial safety.
Keep a part of your monthly income aside to handle unexpected expenses without breaking investments to build an Emergency Fund.
Define short-term goals (vacation, car, emergency fund) and long-term goals (retirement, home, child’s education) to guide your investment choices.
Regular contributions, regardless of market ups and downs, combined with compounding, create long-term wealth.
Revisit your portfolio annually, rebalance if needed, and increase investments as your income grows.
Conclusion
Starting your investment journey with your very first salary is one of the smartest financial decisions you can make and lays a strong foundation for wealth creation. Simple, consistent investments combined with the power of compounding can turn small contributions into significant growth over time. Regular reviews and gradual increases in your investment amount ensure your portfolio evolves with your life. Begin early, stay disciplined, and let time and consistency work in your favour to secure lasting financial freedom.
FAQs
How much of my first salary should I invest?
A practical guideline for managing your first salary is to set aside at least 20% of your net income for investments. If committing the full 20% feels difficult initially, begin with a smaller amount because the earlier you begin, the more you benefit from the power of compounding over time.
Should I save for emergencies before starting SIPs?
Yes, it is wise to create an emergency fund that can cover 3-6 months of essential expenses, such as rent, EMIs, and groceries, before beginning SIPs to ensure long-term investments during unexpected situations and keep you on course with your financial goals.
Is a mutual fund SIP safe for first-time investors?
Yes, Mutual fund SIPs are a smart choice for beginners since they encourage disciplined investing with manageable amounts and minimise the risks of market timing through rupee cost averaging, without investing a large lump sum at once.
What is the best tax-saving option for new salary earners?
The best tax-saving options depend on your investment tenure, risk profile, financial goals, etc. You can choose options eligible for deduction under Section 80C to claim tax benefits on your investments in the old regime.
How do I adjust investments as my salary grows?
If your salary increases by 10%, aim to boost your investments by at least 5%. This way, your lifestyle improves while your wealth-building momentum strengthens.
Disclaimer
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajlifeinsurance.com) carefully before concluding a sale. full_pdf_text:
How to start Investment with your First Salary: Step-by-Step Guide
Why Start Investing from Your First Salary?
Wealth creation rests on the powerful principle of compounding. The sooner you begin investing, the longer your money and the returns it generates can compound. When you invest your first salary, you can let your money start working for you early, giving you a significant advantage in building wealth. So, let’s know how to start investment with first salary.
Step-by-Step Guide: How to Start Investing with Your First Salary
If you are wondering how to start investing your first salary, here are some points that can help -
1. Step 1: Financially Protect Yourself with Life and Health Insurance
Life insurance may not feel urgent when you’re young, healthy, and single, but buying early offers significant long- term advantages. Premiums are much lower when you start young, and a life insurance plan such as a term insurance plan can provide high coverage at an affordable cost. As you move into your 30s, you can reassess your financial needs and adjust your financial plan to protect a growing family or dependent parents. Life insurance acts as a vital safety net, ensuring your loved ones are financially secure if the unexpected happens. A health insurance plan, on the other hand, can cover medical bills and give you financial relief. By investing in life and health insurance early, you can safeguard your family’s future, giving them peace of mind and stability when they need it most. 2. Step 2: Build an Emergency Fund
An emergency fund acts as a buffer against unexpected events such as medical bills, sudden home repairs, or vehicle breakdowns, helping you avoid dipping into savings or taking on debt. This fund ensures you can cover costs without any additional financial strain. Experts suggest maintaining an emergency fund worth a t least 10 times1 your monthly income (subject to individual preferences) to protect your financial stability and gain peace of mind during life’s uncertainties. 3. Step 3: Define Your Short-Term and Long-Term Financial Goals
Investments can be largely divided into two categories based on the purpose behind them:
● Short-term financial goals are those you aim to accomplish within one to three years. Common examples include setting aside money for a vacation, buying a new car, or creating an emergency fund. Since these goals require quick access to funds, they are best supported by low-risk investments that maintain liquidity.
● Long-term financial goals extend beyond three years and often involve larger commitments such as saving for retirement, financing a child’s education, or purchasing a home. Because these goals allow more time to grow, they can benefit
from a more aggressive investment strategy, giving you the ability to weather market ups and downs while building wealth. 4. Step 4: Understand Your Risk Appetite
Understanding your personal risk appetite should be one of the first steps in your investment journey. Evaluating risk means taking into account key financial factors such as inflation, market volatility, interest rates, your investment time horizon, and how your assets are allocated. By identifying your own tolerance for risk, you’ll be better equipped to make informed decisions and choose investments that align with your financial goals and comfort level. 5. Step 5: Decide How Much You Can Invest Every Month
Managing your earnings effectively begins with creating a budget, as it helps you track where your money is going each month. A simple and popular method for beginners is the 50-30-20 rule, which divides your income into three clear categories: 50% for essential needs such as rent, loan EMIs, groceries, and fuel; 30% for personal wants like entertainment, dining out, or leisure activities; and 20% for savings to secure your financial future2. By following this approach, you can strike a healthy balance between meeting daily expenses, enjoying life, and building long-term financial security through different types of investment plans. 6. Step 6: Start with Simple and Goal-Based Investment Options
There are diverse range of investment avenues, each carrying its own balance of risk and potential reward. Gaining a clear understanding of these choices is essential for making smart, well-informed financial decisions. 7. Step 7: Invest Regularly and Stay Consistent
Instead of trying to predict the perfect time to invest, a smarter approach is to keep adding money to your account on a regular basis, whether it’s with every paycheque, once a month, or any other routine that works for you. This steady habit keeps you invested through market ups and downs and takes away the stress of timing the market. Over time, those consistent contributions combine with the power of compounding, where your returns start earning returns of their own. The result is that even small, regular deposits can grow into a substantial amount, making a noticeable difference to your wealth in the long run. 8. Step 8: Review and Increase Investments as your Investments Grow
As your financial goals and priorities evolve, it’s important to keep your investments in sync. Make it a habit to review your portfolio at least once a year to confirm that it still reflects your objectives. If necessary, rebalance your holdings to maintain the asset allocation that best supports your long- term strategy and risk tolerance.
Common Mistakes to Avoid with Your First Investments
Steering clear of the following-mentioned frequent mistakes can significantly boost your long-term financial growth, even if your monthly income is very modest.
1. No Emergency Cushion:
Jumping into investments without first setting aside an emergency fund can backfire. In a crisis, you may be forced to withdraw investments, disrupting the compounding process. 2. Taking Pause During Market Falls:
During market declines, some investment approaches—such as systematic investment
plans (SIPs) or mutual fund purchases—may result in acquiring more units when prices
are lower. Halting contributions during dips means missing out on this advantage.
3. Following Past Performance Blindly:
Picking funds only because they did well previously is a risky move, since past performance doesn’t guarantee of future returns. Focus instead on whether the fund suits your financial goals and risk tolerance. 4. Not Scaling Up Investments:
Keeping the same investment amount for years limits growth. As your income increases, your investment contributions should rise too. 5. Avoid Over‑Diversification to Prevent Portfolio Duplication:
Spreading money across too many varied investments often leads to duplication rather than true diversification. 6. Waiting for the “Perfect” Entry Point:
Delaying investments in hopes of better timing usually results in lost opportunities. Consistency matters more than timing.
Key Takeaways
1. Begin early to harness the power of compounding because time, not timing, is the biggest
wealth creator. 2. Secure life and health insurance early; it’s cheaper when you’re young and ensures your
family’s financial safety. 3. Keep a part of your monthly income aside to handle unexpected expenses without breaking
investments to build an Emergency Fund. 4. Define short-term goals (vacation, car, emergency fund) and long-term goals (retirement,
home, child’s education) to guide your investment choices. 5. Regular contributions, regardless of market ups and downs, combined with compounding,
create long-term wealth. 6. Revisit your portfolio annually, rebalance if needed, and increase investments as your
income grows.
Conclusion
Starting your investment journey with your very first salary is one of the smartest financial decisions you can make and lays a strong foundation for wealth creation. Simple, consistent investments combined with the power of compounding can turn small contributions into significant growth over time. Regular reviews and gradual increases in your investment amount ensure your portfolio evolves with your life. Begin early, stay disciplined, and let time and consistency work in your favour to secure lasting financial freedom.
FAQs
1. How much of my first salary should I invest?
A practical guideline for managing your first salary is to set aside at least 20%3 of your net income for savings and investments. If committing the full 20% feels difficult initially, begin with a smaller amount because the earlier you begin, the more you benefit from the power of compounding over time. 2. Should I save for emergencies before starting SIPs?
Yes, it is wise to create an emergency fund that can cover 3-6 months5 of essential expenses, such as rent, EMIs, and groceries, before beginning SIPs to ensure long-term investments during unexpected situations and keep you on course with your financial goals. 3. Is a mutual fund SIP safe for first-time investors?
Yes, Mutual fund SIPs are a smart choice for beginners since they encourage disciplined investing with manageable amounts and minimise the risks of market timing, without investing a large lump sum at once. 4. What is the best tax-saving option for new salary earners?
The best tax-saving options depend on your investment tenure, risk profile, financial goals, etc. You can choose options eligible for deduction under Section 80C to claim tax benefits on your investments in the old regime. 5. How do I adjust investments as my salary grows?
If your salary increases by 10%, aim to boost your investments by at least 5%4. This way, your lifestyle improves while your wealth-building momentum strengthens.
Disclaimer
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajlifeinsurance.com) carefully before concluding a sale. Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility

your-savings 3. https://www.finedge.in/blog/correct-investing-practices/how-to-invest-your-first-
salary#:~:text=1.,can%20reduce%20your%20saving%20potential 4. https://www.wealthredefine.in/the-best-investment-plan-for-a-30k-salary/ 5. https://www.morganstanley.com/articles/how-to-build-an-emergency-fund
